Although the benefits of opening the questions might seem obvious now, it took the business world and the FBI a while to realize how effective they really are. A major problem we encountered was the obligations of reciprocity. It's a universal rule across all cultures, all of them. When you enter into any negotiation, their scene is tit for tat type situations. When someone gives you something, they expect something in return. Not at all unlike your world. And like your world, they won't forget until you pay them back. I know the stakes are different, but the feelings are the same. Say you called up the hostage takers and asked for proof of life. Well, they would expect something in return. This is dangerous because if you fail to make good on a debt, then you've engaged in bad faith negotiations. Tensions flare, people feel betrayed, the entire negotiation breaks down. In my former world, this could result in loss of life. In your world, it's the loss of a deal. So knowing this mentality, we'd limit calling for proof of life because we've got to be careful about going into debt. Even more, we never asked to speak to the hostages directly because we felt we knew the hostage takers would say no. So we would effectively be negotiating against ourselves before we even got to the proverbial table. As a result, we limited our own ability to gain the upper hand. So when it ended up happening, well, since we didn't want to trigger the human reciprocity gene, our negotiations took longer than necessary. Each side wanted to extract something but wasn't capable of doing so without making some sort of concession. Eventually, I realized that what we had been doing was more ineffective verbal posturing than actually making strategic negotiation progress. As you might surmise, this is a tough position to be in. How do we get proof of life, get the chance to speak directly to the hostage and stay debt-free all at the same time? The first step is to suspend the active resistance to hearing what the other side is saying. Suspend their defense mechanisms. Get them to lower their guard. This is what psychologist Kevin Dutton calls unbelief and is essentially the automatic rejection of your counterparts' perspective in order to preserve your own. Unfortunately, almost every single negotiation begins on this dynamic and when the conversation stays on this course, you end up having a showdown with the other party. Like two rams fighting for position, just headbutting each other until one side gets exhausted and submits. However, if you can deactivate your counterparts' unbelief, you get them to drop their barriers and slowly work themselves to see things your way on the back of their own energy. Your job as a negotiator isn't to counter all their points with an elegant argument that proves them wrong in a vain attempt to get them to see it from your side of the fence. Why? Because arguments, elegance or no, just don't work. Arguments are intellectual self-stimulation and that should be a sufficient description to illustrate their ability to produce actual outcomes. You can't plant in your counterparts' brain what you see as the one obvious correct answer by asking closed-ended questions. You get their brains working with thought-provoking, open-ended, calibrated ones. These types of questions cause the other party to take a pause and actually consider how best to solve the problem. Best of all, it isn't a demand or request. You won't give them a single thing because it's a normal and natural conversational question. Therefore, when they come up with a solution, they will believe it was their idea. Our job is easier than you might think. Let me let you in on a little secret. You don't have to get others to believe in what you say. You just need to get them to stop unbelieving. That's half the game right there. Posing an idea properly in a form of calibrated questions is one of the most powerful and important tools you will have at your disposal to suspend unbelief. Before we conclude this chapter, I want to leave you with one of the greatest calibrated questions of all time. Next time you're negotiating and the offer isn't where you'd hoped it would be, just ask, how am I supposed to do that? Your delivery must convey that you really are asking for their help. When you use this question, you'll transform encounters from confrontational showdowns into joint problem-solving sessions where they feel like they're in control while simultaneously giving you the upper hand in the conversation.
